TITLE: Design an API Rate Limiter
DOMAIN: ratelimiter

62	- **Types**: `List<Foo>`, `Map<K, V>`, `Set<T>`, `@Nullable X`, `Optional<X>` for return types
63	- **Immutable entities**: `record Foo(int id, String name) {}` (Java 17+)
64	- **Exceptions**: Extend `RuntimeException` (unchecked), one base per domain
65	- **Concurrency**:
66	  - `ReentrantLock` (java.util.concurrent.locks)
67	  - `Semaphore` (java.util.concurrent)
68	  - `CyclicBarrier` (java.util.concurrent)
69	  - Lock pattern: `lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); }`
70	- **Threading**: `ExecutorService exec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(N); exec.submit(() -> ...); exec.shutdown(); exec.awaitTermination(...);`

72	### Testing (JUnit 5)
73	```java
74	import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
75	import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;
76	
77	class ResourcePoolServiceTest {
78	    @Test
79	    void testAcquire() {
80	        var pool = new ResourcePoolService(...);
81	        var resource = pool.acquire(Duration.ofSeconds(5));
82	        assertNotNull(resource);
83	    }
84	    
85	    @Test
86	    void testExhaustion() {
87	        var pool = new ResourcePoolService(..., maxSize: 1);
88	        pool.acquire(null);
89	        assertThrows(PoolExhaustedException.class, () -> pool.acquire(Duration.ofMillis(50)));
90	    }
91	}
92	```
93	
94	### Concurrency Test (Strict)
95	```java
96	int N = 50, CAP = 5;
97	CyclicBarrier barrier = new CyclicBarrier(N);
98	AtomicInteger served = new AtomicInteger(0);
99	AtomicInteger peak = new AtomicInteger(0);
100	Set<String> distinct = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();
101	
102	ExecutorService exec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(N);
103	for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
104	    exec.submit(() -> {
105	        try {
106	            barrier.await();  // stampede
107	            try (var r = pool.acquire(Duration.ofSeconds(10))) {
108	                served.incrementAndGet();
109	                distinct.add(r.getPayload().toString());
110	                int cur = pool.inUseCount();
111	                peak.updateAndGet(p -> Math.max(p, cur));
112	                assertTrue(cur <= CAP, "capacity breached");
113	                Thread.sleep(20);  // hold to force genuine concurrency
114	            }
115	        } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
116	    });
117	}
118	exec.shutdown();
119	assertTrue(exec.awaitTermination(15, TimeUnit.SECONDS));
120	
121	assertEquals(N, served.get(), "all threads served");
122	assertEquals(CAP, peak.get(), "pool actually filled to capacity");
123	assertTrue(distinct.size() <= CAP, "no more than CAP resources created");
124	```

149	mvn compile exec:java -q   # Main.java all cases print OK

151	```
152	
153	If red, fix CODE (not test) and rerun. Update §7.1 / §7.2 in `_response.md` with actual output.
154	
155	## Distinct Concurrency Angle (Per-Problem)
156	
157	Each problem must realize a DIFFERENT concurrency mechanism (no two use the exact same pattern):
158	- Lock ordering (deadlock-free)
159	- Lock striping (per-customer shards)
160	- Permit-guarded gate (Semaphore + lazy creation)
161	- Idempotent dedup (concurrent seen-set, work outside lock)
162	- Fair FIFO (Semaphore + queue)
163	- Optimistic CAS loop
164	- Per-resource locks (fine-grained)

Throughout, add 💬 blockquote lines (start the line with `> 💬`) giving the exact, natural words to say — conversational, first-person, not bookish. For any hard-to-articulate or jargon-heavy concept, ALSO give a simple plain-English ("layman") way to explain it, in addition to the precise technical statement. 52 53 54 55 56 You have a web_search tool. Use it when current, real-world facts would improve the answer (modern best practices, real system scale numbers, specific library/API behaviour, recent tech). Don't guess on time-sensitive specifics — search, then state briefly what you found. 57 58 59 60 61 This is a multi-turn session. After this first complete answer the user will ask follow-ups (often dictated by voice) — answer them directly and concisely. If the user asks to CHANGE, fix, or extend the solution, output the UPDATED complete solution (clearly, so the newest message is always the current best version). For plain questions, just answer conversationally without repeating everything. 62 63 64 65 66 Engineering rigor — apply to EVERY problem (these prevent the classic LLD mistakes) 67 68 1. MODEL THE DOMAIN TRUTHFULLY — never lose information. Identify the core invariants and preserve them end to end. Do NOT collapse a rich/typed request or event into a bare primitive that drops attributes (e.g. direction, type, priority, owner, timestamp, requested-resource). If an attribute matters at decision time it MUST survive to execution time. 69 2. THE ORCHESTRATOR OWNS THE TRUTH. The controller/manager/service keeps a registry of in-flight requests/resources it can enumerate, look up, cancel, retry, reassign and recover. Never bury the only copy of pending state where it can't be reached, or where one component going offline silently strands it. 70 3. EVERY REQUEST HAS A DEFINED OUTCOME — accept, queue, or reject-with-reason. NEVER silently drop one. Always handle the "no resource available / all busy / not found" path explicitly (queue + retry, or reject). A returned null/None that loses the request is a bug. 71 4. VALIDATE AT EVERY PUBLIC BOUNDARY — reject out-of-range values, unknown ids, nulls and contradictory requests with a clear error BEFORE mutating any state. 72 5. DATA STRUCTURES FIT THE OPERATIONS — choose structures that make the core operations direct instead of re-deriving state every step; distinguish "committed" vs "served/completed" where the lifecycle needs it. 73 6. CONCURRENCY: ONE MODEL, APPLIED CONSISTENTLY. If anything is shared or concurrent: (a) state the model — single-threaded event loop, OR one thread/actor per entity, OR a lock-guarded shared object; (b) guard ALL shared mutable reads AND writes, not just some; (c) make check-then-act sequences atomic (e.g. select-and-assign together); (d) use a reentrant lock if a guarded method may call another guarded method, and say so; (e) never claim a concurrency model you did not actually implement. 74 7. RESOURCE LIMITS & LIFECYCLE EDGES — model capacity/limits and the full/exhausted behaviour; handle entity removal or failure mid-operation, duplicates, empty, single, and maximum. 75 8. STATE THE OBJECTIVE & FAIRNESS — name the optimization objective and key non-functional requirements; guard against starvation/unfairness where relevant. 76 9. EARN YOUR PATTERNS & PRINCIPLES — only claim a design pattern or SOLID principle if it is actually realized in the code you show. If you say "extensible via Observer/Strategy", wire the seam. Do not overstate SOLID. 77 10. DOMAIN SAFETY / SPECIAL MODES — enumerate the domain's failure, safety and special modes (emergency, overload, timeout, degraded, cancellation, recovery) and model the important ones as first-class, not afterthoughts. 78 79 80 81 82 These are moves that take an answer from "hire" to "strong hire." They tell the interviewer: "this person has built real systems, not just studied for interviews." 83 84 IMPORTANT — this is a toolbox, not a checklist. For each problem, pick the ones that actually fit. Using all 8 on a Chess problem would look forced and wrong. Also: after picking from this list, ask yourself what the "smart move" is SPECIFIC TO THIS PROBLEM — every LLD has one non-obvious insight the interviewer most wants to hear. State it clearly (in §5.5-A) and make the code reflect it. 85 86 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 87 MOVE 1: Control Time 88 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 89 Pass time in as a parameter — `now_fn=datetime.now` — instead of calling `datetime.now()` directly inside your code. 90 91 Why this matters: anything that depends on time (holds that expire, sessions that time out, reservations) becomes testable without actually waiting. In the test, you just change what "now" means. 92 93 ```python 94 # In the service: 95 def __init__(self, ..., now_fn=datetime.now): 96 self._now = now_fn # tests will pass in a fake clock 97 98 # In the test: 99 clock = {"t": start_time} 100 svc = BookingService(now_fn=lambda: clock["t"]) 101 clock["t"] = start_time + timedelta(minutes=10) # jump forward — no sleep needed 102 ``` 103 104 What to say: "I pass the clock in as a function so I can test expiry without actually waiting 5 minutes. In the test I just move the clock forward." 105 106 Use when: the problem has holds, timeouts, reservations, or any time limit. 107 108 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 109 MOVE 2: A Do-Nothing Lock That Can Be Swapped 110 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 111 Write a method `_lock(resource)` on your main class that does nothing at all. In the thread-safe version (a subclass), override it with a real lock. 112 113 Why this matters: your main code has zero lock-related code. The thread-safe version changes only this one small method. The interviewer sees clearly: "clean version → locked version." The difference is about 10 lines. 114 115 ```python 116 # Main class — does nothing, no locks anywhere in this file: 117 @contextmanager 118 def _lock(self, resource) -> Iterator[None]: 119 yield # does nothing — subclass will override this 120 121 # Thread-safe subclass — only this method changes: 122 @contextmanager 123 def _lock(self, resource) -> Iterator[None]: 124 with self._locks[resource.id]: 125 yield 126 ``` 127 128 What to say: "My main code has no lock imports at all. The thread-safe version is a subclass that only overrides this one method. The diff is about 10 lines." 129 130 Use when: any problem that asks about concurrency (which is every LLD problem). 131 132 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 133 MOVE 3: Lock the Map Before You Lock the Thing 134 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 135 If you keep one lock per resource in a dictionary, lock the dictionary itself before reading from it — using a separate lock. 136 137 Why this matters: two threads creating a new key in a dict at the same time can corrupt the dict itself. The map needs its own lock. 138 139 ```python 140 with self._map_lock: # Step 1: lock the dictionary 141 lock = self._locks[id] # Step 2: get the per-resource lock 142 with lock: # Step 3: lock the actual resource 143 yield 144 ``` 145 146 What to say: "I use two locks — one to protect the dictionary of locks, one for each resource. Without the first lock, two threads adding a new resource at the same time could break the dictionary." 147 148 Use when: you have one lock per resource stored in a dictionary. 149 150 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 151 MOVE 4: A Fake That Can Fail Once Then Succeed 152 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 153 Besides an always-success and always-fail fake, write one that returns results from a list in order. This lets you test "fails first time, succeeds second time." 154 155 ```python 156 class ScriptedFakeGateway(PaymentGateway): 157 def __init__(self, results): # e.g. [FAILURE, SUCCESS] 158 self._results = list(results) 159 def charge(self, amount): 160 if len(self._results) > 1: 161 return self._results.pop(0) # use first, remove it 162 return self._results[0] # last one keeps repeating 163 ``` 164 165 What to say: "This fake lets me control exactly what each payment attempt returns. I use it to test the retry flow — fail first, succeed second." 166 167 Use when: the problem has anything that can fail and be retried (payment, sending a notification, calling an external service). 168 169 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 170 MOVE 5: Make Everyone Race at the Same Moment 171 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 172 In your concurrency test, make all threads wait at a starting line before going. Without this, threads just take turns — there is no real race. 173 174 ```python 175 N = 50 176 start = threading.Barrier(N) # all N threads wait here together 177 178 def attempt(user): 179 session = svc.start_session(user, resource) 180 start.wait() # wait until all N are ready, then go 181 try: 182 svc.claim(session.id, ["A1"]) # now all N hit at the same time 183 except ResourceNotAvailable: 184 pass 185 ``` 186 187 What to say: "The Barrier makes all 50 threads hit the critical part at the exact same moment. Without it, they just go one after another — not a real race." 188 189 Use when: writing a test to prove no double-booking or no double-allocation. 190 191 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 192 MOVE 6: Lock Objects That Should Never Change 193 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 194 Objects that are "done" after they are created — a confirmed ticket, a movie, a seat layout, a user — should be marked frozen. Python will throw an error if anything tries to change them by accident. 195 196 ```python 197 @dataclass(frozen=True) # Python stops you if you try to change this 198 class Ticket: 199 id: str 200 seats: tuple[Seat, ...] 201 amount: int 202 203 @dataclass # this one changes (seat gets held, released, confirmed) 204 class ShowSeat: 205 status: SeatStatus 206 ``` 207 208 What to say: "I mark completed objects as frozen — once a ticket is issued, nothing can change it. Python enforces this automatically." 209 210 Use when: any object that should not change after it is created (tickets, moves in Chess, completed orders, confirmed bookings). 211 212 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 213 MOVE 7: Expose a Cleanup Method for the Background Timer 214 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 215 Write a `sweep_expired()` or `release_stale()` method on your main class. Write a comment saying "a background timer thread calls this every minute." 216 217 Why this matters: it shows you have thought about what happens in a real running system, not just the happy path. Real systems need something to clean up holds and reservations that were never completed. 218 219 ```python 220 def sweep_expired(self) -> None: 221 # a background timer thread calls this to release holds that timed out 222 for resource in self.resources.values(): 223 with self._lock(resource): 224 self._release_stale(resource) 225 ``` 226 227 What to say: "I make cleanup an explicit method — in production, a timer calls this every minute to release any holds that the user never finished." 228 229 Use when: the problem has holds, reservations, or sessions that can time out. 230 231 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 232 MOVE 8: Numbered IDs for Easy Testing 233 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 234 Use a counter for IDs (`sess-1`, `tkt-2`) instead of random UUIDs. Numbered IDs make them simple. 235 236 ```python 237 self._counter = itertools.count(1) 238 def _make_id(self, prefix): return f"{prefix}-{next(self._counter)}" 239 ``` 240 241 What to say: "I use a counter for IDs in tests — `tkt-1`, `tkt-2` — so assertions are easy to read. In production I would switch to UUID." 242 243 Use always (but note the production trade-off). 244 245 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 246 THE MOST IMPORTANT INSTRUCTION — The One Smart Insight for THIS Problem 247 ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 248 Every LLD problem has one non-obvious insight that is specific to that problem. Use it sparingly, max once per interview. 439 2. **Core operations confirmation** — propose the 2-3 main user actions and ask if that's the full set. Phrasing: "Sounds like the core flow is A, B, C — anything else the system needs to support, or is that the v1 surface?" 440 3. **Scope cuts** — proactively name things to cut. Phrasing: "I'd assume [logistics / notification delivery / payments / UI rendering / multi-region] is out of scope for this round — agreed?" Most interviewers happily agree because it shows judgment. 441 4. **Edge case policy** — propose how to HANDLE the trickiest edge, don't ask "what should happen?". Phrasing: "If all compartments are full, I'd just reject the request with a typed error — no queueing — sound right?" or "I'd say expired codes get rejected on use, package stays put until staff sweeps it — OK?" 442 5. **Constraints with sensible defaults** — propose a number/policy. Phrasing: "I'll assume codes expire after ~7 days, one code per package, 6-digit numeric. Fine for now?" 443 6. **Failure-mode contract** — propose what the system does on partial failure. Design Patterns & Principles 638 639 The goal here is NOT to list every pattern you know — it's to name the SMALL number of patterns that actually shape THIS design and explain WHY each one earned its keep. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix 736 Don't list "tricky things" as random bullets. Each entry has THREE labelled parts so the candidate can defend it under interview pressure: 737 738 **Format for each:** 739 > **🧠 Claim:** [what the tricky thing is, in one sentence] > **🔬 Proof:** [the SPECIFIC sequence of events that exposes the bug if you do it wrong] > **🛠️ Fix in code:** [the exact line / mechanism in §6 that handles it] 740 741 Example: 742 > **🧠 Claim:** `release()` must be idempotent — calling it on an already-AVAILABLE > compartment must be a no-op, not an error. 743 > 744 > **🔬 Proof:** The expiry sweep and a real pickup can race. Both decide the reservation > is done; one calls `release()` first and flips the box AVAILABLE; the second arrives > and would normally hit "illegal transition AVAILABLE → AVAILABLE" — except it'd > wrongly flag a fault on the legitimate pickup path. 745 > 746 > **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Compartment.release()` checks `if self.status in (RESERVED, > OCCUPIED): self.status = AVAILABLE` — outside that set, it returns silently. The > 50-thread test in §7/§9 exercises this exact race. 747 748 Cover 4-6 such tricky points. Race conditions, off-by-one in expiry windows, idempotency, deterministic ordering (why sort by `(size, id)` not just `size`), injected clocks, defensive copies. Each one labelled 🧠 / 🔬 / 🛠️. 749 750 ### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example (one only) 751 Pick the SINGLE step a junior would get wrong, write the wrong version in 2-3 lines of pseudocode, and explain in one sentence WHY it's wrong. This shows you've internalised the failure mode, not just the success path. Example: 752 753 > **❌ Naive version a junior would write:** 754 > ``` 755 > def assign_package(self, locker_id, package): 756 > compartment = self.find_available(locker_id, package.size) # bare lookup 757 > compartment.status = CompartmentStatus.RESERVED # direct write 758 > return Reservation(...) 759 > ``` 760 > **Why it's wrong:** Two failure modes. (1) Direct status write bypasses the state > machine — illegal transitions become possible. (2) The find-then-write is check-then-act > — under concurrency, two callers can both see "available" and both reserve. The > correct version delegates to a Strategy, calls `compartment.reserve()` (so the entity > guards itself), and lifts the whole block under a per-locker lock in §9. 761 762 ONE anti-example for the most critical method. More than one becomes noise. 763 764 ### F. Narration Order for §6 (with method-level open lines) 765 State the exact order in which to present classes to the interviewer, and write the ONE sentence that opens each. The 🎙️ open lines in §6 should literally come from here — don't write them twice. Format: 766 767 | Order | Class | One-line open (literal — say this out loud as you start it) | 768 |---|---|---| 769 | 1 | Enums | "Let me start with the vocabulary — every state in the system, in one place." | 770 | 2 | `Compartment` (state machine) | "This is the heart — the only class allowed to change a compartment's status." | 771 | 3 | `AllocationStrategy` interface + `BestFitAllocation` | "The seam — the orchestrator never knows which policy is wired in." | 772 | 4 | `LockerService.__init__` + skeleton | "Here's the brain. Two indexes — by id for the sweep, by code for O(1) pickup." | 773 | 5 | `LockerService.assign_package` | "Now the interesting part — this is the pipeline I dry-ran above." | 774 | 6 | `LockerService.pickup_package` | "Pickup is three guards and a release. The one-time-ness is the pop on the code map." | 775 776 The open lines here become the literal `🎙️ Script` headers in §6 — never reword them. This eliminates the "every script sounds the same" problem because §5.5.F authors them ONCE, in a varied voice, and §6 just reuses them. 777 778 ## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class 779 Write the BEST version of this code you can — production-quality, not interview-sloppy: clean and idiomatic, FULLY TYPE-HINTED (Python 3.11+ syntax: `List`, `@Nullable X`), small cohesive classes, precise names, a short docstring per class, **no dead code**, specific error types (every exception extends a single domain base — never bare `Exception`/`RuntimeError`/`ValueError` mixed in), and the design patterns from §5 VISIBLY wired in (a real Strategy interface with implementations, a real factory, etc.). Keep it genuinely MODULAR per using the CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT — `model/*.java`, `strategies.java` (if needed), `gateways.java` (if needed), `_service.java`, `main.java`, plus `tests/test_.java`. Never invent new filenames. This is the CLEAN, single-threaded-correct CORE — do NOT add any locks or thread-safety here; concurrency hardening is a deliberate SECOND version in §9. Keep §6/§7 lock-free and readable so the OO design is the star; §7's run proves FUNCTIONAL correctness only. 780 781 PRESENTATION — METHOD BY METHOD, not class by class. Inside the orchestrator, do NOT dump the whole class as one ```java block. Instead, for the orchestrator: 782 1. First show `__init__` + the method signatures (skeleton, with `...` bodies for the methods you'll fill in below). This is the API tour — interviewer sees the full surface area in one glance. 783 2. Then for EACH non-trivial method, present its OWN small ```java block followed by the 4-block format below (role / code / 🎙️ narration / ⚠️ follow-up). 784 785 The skeleton-then-flesh approach matches how the candidate would actually write the class at a whiteboard, and it makes the rendered output scannable instead of one wall of code. Small entity classes (`Compartment`, `Package`, `Reservation`, `Locker`) can still be presented as a single ```java block per file because their methods are short and tightly coupled — the per-method split is only required for the orchestrator. 786 787 **LIVE WRITING ORDER — the candidate's playbook for the live interview itself.** 788 789 This is the BIG mental-model section. The candidate cannot write the §6 code in any order they want — there's a specific phased sequence that maximises interviewer signal and minimises wasted time. ALWAYS render this as a sub-section titled "### 6.0 Live Writing Order (your interview playbook)" BEFORE any code blocks. The candidate reads this OUT LOUD pacing in their head while writing — it's their clock. Total target: ~30 minutes of code time inside a 60-minute interview. 790 791 The section has three parts in order: (a) the 9 phases with what-to-say + what-to-write, (b) the 7 golden rules, (c) a TL;DR time budget table. All three are mandatory. 792 793 #### (a) The 9 phases 794 795 Present each phase as: **Phase title + time budget** → bold "Bolo first" line (what to SAY) → then "Likho:" with a tiny representative code snippet (sub-5-line) so the candidate knows what to write at that step. The snippets are illustrative TEMPLATES adapted to THIS problem (Locker / ParkingLot / etc.) — not full re-renders of §6 code. 796 797 **Phase 1 — Set the stage (1-2 min). NO CODE YET.** 798 > Bolo first: "Main `.java`, `.java`, aur `.java` — ye 3 > files banaunga. Pehle states define karunga (enums), phir `` > ki state machine, phir orchestrator. Code likhne se pehle main aapko har class ka > role bata deta hoon." 799 > Why this matters: 30 seconds of framing tells the interviewer you're not in > chaos. Roadmap pehle, code baad mein. 800 801 **Phase 2 — Enums first (2 min). Quick win.** 802 > Bolo while writing: "States pehle — har class ki vocabulary yahaan se aati hai. > `IntEnum` use kar raha hoon size ke liye taaki 'fits' check ek line ho jaaye." 803 > Likho: 1 small ```java block with the IntEnum + 1-2 plain Enums. No methods. 804 > Why this matters: psychological — first green checkmark on the board, momentum > established. 805 806 **Phase 3 — Most-interesting entity state machine (5 min).** 807 > Bolo: "Ye system ka heart hai. Sirf yeh class apna status badal sakti hai — > orchestrator kabhi seedha status set nahi karega. Isse illegal jumps impossible > ho jaate hain by construction." 808 > Likho: the entity dataclass + every state-transition method (`reserve`, `release`, > `occupy` for Locker; `hold`, `book`, `cancel` for Seat; etc.). Idempotent guards > shown explicitly. 809 > Then immediately drop the **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**: "Yahan ek race > condition hogi `` mein — main usko §9 mein per-resource lock se > fix karunga. Abhi clean version likhta hoon." This single sentence is the > biggest senior signal in the whole interview. 810 811 **Phase 4 — Strategy interface + 1 concrete (1-2 min).** 812 > Bolo: "Strategy seam — orchestrator ko nahi pata best-fit hai ya nearest-door." 813 > Likho: ABC + abstractmethod + 1 concrete impl (~6 lines). 814 > If the design has no Strategy pattern, SKIP this phase entirely — don't fabricate one. 815 816 **Phase 5 — Orchestrator SKELETON (2-3 min).** ⚠️ CRITICAL. 817 > Bolo: "Pehle main saari API surface dikha deta hoon. Phir do main methods bharta hoon." 818 > Likho: ONLY `__init__` (with all fields, all injected dependencies) + EVERY public > method signature with `pass` body. Do NOT fill any body yet. 819 > Why this matters: interviewer sees the full API in one glance. If the candidate > dives straight into `assign_package`'s body, the interviewer waits anxiously > wondering what other methods exist. Skeleton answers that question once and for all. 820 821 **Phase 6 — Most important method, FULL body (5-7 min). Slow down here.** 822 > Bolo step-by-step (this is the most important 5 min of the interview). Walk > through guard clauses → critical block → notify-outside-lock → return. 823 > Likho: the full body of the orchestrator's pipeline method (`assign_package`, > `park`, `bookSeats`, `addExpense`). Include the no-op `_lock` context manager > marking the §9 boundary. Notify call OUTSIDE the lock. 824 > Narration must call out: "Notify lock ke BAAHAR hai — agar SMS slow hai to lock > mat block karo." This one sentence is a senior signal. 825 826 **Phase 7 — Second method, FULL body (3-4 min).** 827 > Bolo: "Ye redemption / completion path hai. guards, phir release." 828 > Likho: full body of `pickup_package` / `unpark` / `settle` etc. Reference §5.5.B > pseudocode 1:1 — no improvisation, just translation. 829 830 **Phase 8 — Demo verbally OR run (3-4 min).** 831 > Mode A (Amazon-style, no execution): "Main 3 cases verbally walk karunga: > happy path, capacity-full, edge case." Then SHOW state transitions in plain > text on the board: 832 > ``` 833 > assign(SMALL pkg) → C1: AVAILABLE → RESERVED, _by_code={"9421": res-1} 834 > deposit(res-1) → C1: RESERVED → OCCUPIED 835 > pickup("9421") → C1: OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE, _by_code={} 836 > ``` 837 > Mode B (Uber/Meta coding env): `mvn compile exec:java -Dexec.mainClass="com.interview.lld..Main" -q`, show actual stdout. 838 > Wait until ALL methods are full-body before this step. A demo with one half-written > method is worse than no demo. 839 840 **Phase 9 — Concurrency (2-3 min, ONLY if asked).** 841 > Bolo first, ALWAYS: "Race `` mein hai — do callers same resource > dekh ke dono reserve kar dete hain. Fix: per-resource RLock through Template > Method — `_lock` hook ko subclass override karta hai, core code zero changes." 842 > Likho the thread-safe subclass ONLY if interviewer says "show me the code". > Otherwise stay verbal. 843 844 #### (b) The 7 golden rules — must be rendered as a numbered list 845 846 1. **Roadmap pehle bolo.** "Main 3 files banaunga, A → B → C." 30 seconds of talking saves the interviewer's anxiety and frames everything that follows. 847 848 2. **Skeleton-then-flesh.** Orchestrator gets `pass` bodies first, full bodies later. Never write the orchestrator class as one ```java blob. 849 850 3. **State machine before orchestrator.** Compartment / Seat / Spot — write these first. They're independent; orchestrator imports them. 851 852 4. **BOLO while likhing.** Silence = interviewer's brain wanders. Even a one-liner helps: "yahan clock inject kar raha hoon", "yeh guard rejection path hai". 853 854 5. **Concurrency flag drop karo entity ke baad.** Proactively, before the interviewer asks. Single biggest senior signal of the whole hour. 855 856 6. **Demo wait karo.** Even one half-written method = no demo. Show progress only once everything runs. 857 858 7. **Pseudocode → code 1:1.** §5.5.B's numbered logic becomes §6's code in the SAME order. Don't improvise live — translate the dry-run. 859 860 #### (c) TL;DR time budget table — render exactly this shape 861 862 | Time | Phase | What you're writing | 863 |---|---|---| 864 | 0-2 min | Roadmap | Just talking, no code | 865 | 2-4 min | Enums | Full enums | 866 | 4-9 min | State-machine entity | Full state machine class + methods | 867 | 9-11 min | Strategy seam | ABC + 1 concrete (skip if no Strategy in §5) | 868 | 11-14 min | Orchestrator skeleton | `__init__` + signatures with `pass` | 869 | 14-21 min | Pipeline method (`assign` / `park` / `book`) | Full body + narration | 870 | 21-25 min | Redemption method (`pickup` / `unpark` / `settle`) | Full body + narration | 871 | 25-29 min | Demo / verbal trace | 3 cases |

872	| 29-32 min | Concurrency answer | Verbal first, code only if asked |
873	
874	Total: ~30 minutes of code time. In a 60-min interview, allocate 25 min for §1-§5 (talking + diagram), 30 min for §6 code, 5 min for follow-ups.
875	
876	#### (d) Mid-flight situations — when things don't go to plan
877	
878	Every interview hits at least one of these. The candidate must know the response before the situation arises — improvising under pressure usually makes it worse. Render this as a numbered list of 5 situations, each with a one-line response.
879	
880	  1. **Interviewer interrupts mid-method.** STOP writing. Don't try to talk and write simultaneously — both will suffer. Address the question fully, then say "OK, where was I — I was in the middle of `<method>`, let me finish the lock block." Resume from the same line. Common mistake: keeping the cursor moving while answering → you'll write something nonsensical.
881	
882	  2. **Running out of time at the 40-min mark.** Cut in this EXACT order, never skip an earlier item to preserve a later one:
883	  > - **First cut:** second method body — describe verbally instead. "I'll describe `<second_method>` instead of writing it — same shape, three guards then release."
884	  > - **Second cut:** §6 demo / verbal trace — skip if you've narrated dry-runs in §5.5.
885	  > - **Third cut:** §9 code — stay verbal, never write the thread-safe subclass under time pressure.
886	  > - **NEVER cut:** state machine, pipeline method body, proactive concurrency flag. These three ARE the senior signal — losing any one costs more than overrunning by 2-3 min.
887	
888	  3. **Realized you need a missing field / method mid-write.** Pause for 3 seconds, voice it out loud — "actually I need a `created_at` on Reservation, let me add it" — then add it inline. DO NOT scroll back and silently edit; interviewer reads that as confusion. Voicing it reads as deliberate refinement.
889	
890	  4. **Interviewer says "what if X?" while you're mid-method.** Two valid responses, pick by scope:
891	  > - **Defer:** "Good question — let me finish this method, then show how the design handles that." Use when X is a §9 / follow-up topic.
892	  > - **Pivot inline:** "Actually that changes things — let me add a parameter here." Use when X is a small inline tweak (one arg, one status).
893	  > NEVER just say "yes that works" without knowing why — you'll paint yourself into a corner one method later.
894	
895	  5. **Interviewer silence.** Don't fill it with chatter — keep working at a steady pace. At natural breakpoints (after a method's full body), pause and ask: "any thoughts before I move to the next method?" Gives them a clean opening to redirect, without breaking your flow.
896	
897	End the §6.0 sub-section with a one-line takeaway:
898	> "Yahi flow practice karo. Sequence muscle-memory ban jaayegi: > entity → strategy → skeleton → main method → second method → demo. > Interview mein flow natural lagega."
899	
900	#### After §6.0 — what NOT to write
901	
902	🗣️ **DESCRIBE (don't write unless asked):** Simple entity classes (Show, Movie, Theatre, User, Ticket), simple factory/helper methods, basic exception classes. Say: "I also have [ClassName] — it just holds [fields], nothing interesting there."
903	
904	❓ **IF ASKED (write only if interviewer specifically requests it):** Thread-safe wrapper, sweep_expired/cleanup method, observer registration, main.java driver. These are §9 territory — don't pre-emptively write them.
905	
906	NARRATION ORDER — always follow the live writing order in §6.0 AND the §5.5-F roadmap. Open §6 with the one-liner 🎙️ Script from §5.5-F so the interviewer immediately knows the roadmap.
907	
908	For each ✍️ WRITE method (on the orchestrator), present ALL SIX of these in order — this is the mandatory tutorial-style format. Hellointerview reads as if a senior engineer is THINKING ALOUD; our output must read the same way.
909	
910	  **1. Role sentence:** One sentence — WHY this method exists, not just what it does.   "`depositPackage` is the core deposit workflow — it ties a compartment to a token   in one atomic step."
911	
912	  **2. Core logic (bulleted happy path):** A small numbered list of the steps the   method takes on the happy path, BEFORE any code appears. The reader scans this and   is mentally ready by the time they see the code. Format:
913	  > Core logic:
914	  > 1. Find an available compartment of the requested size
915	  > 2. Generate an access token for that compartment
916	  > 3. Mark the compartment as occupied
917	  > 4. Store the access token in the lookup map
918	  > 5. Return the access token code
919	
920	  **3. Edge cases (bulleted):** A small list of what can go wrong, written as nouns:
921	  > Edge cases:
922	  > - No compartment available of the requested size
923	  > - Invalid size parameter
924	  > - Code generated already exists (collision)
925	
926	  **4. Code block:** ```java — implementing EXACTLY the §5.5-B pseudocode.   ⚠️ NO `...` or `pass` as body placeholders anywhere in §6 code — every method must   have a real, complete, runnable body. Comment the one or two non-obvious lines inline   using `# WHY:`. Don't comment obvious lines.
927	
928	  **5. Reasoning paragraph (PROSE, not teleprompter):** 3-6 sentences of plain-English   explanation written as a senior engineer reasoning aloud — NOT a "say this out loud"   script. This is the longest piece in the method's section and must include:
929	  - **Open** with what this method is doing at a high level — one sentence — and refer to the most-interesting line by name (`compartment.open()` triggers the unlock; the `pop` burns the one-time code).
930	  - **At least one "Notice" sentence** about a deliberate non-decision — "Notice we're NOT checking if size is valid here — that's the job of `getAvailableCompartment`, which will scan and return None for unknown sizes." Senior signal: explicitly named choices.
931	  - **Production-vs-interview boundary** when applicable — "We assume the hardware auto-closes the door after ~30 seconds. In production we'd add physical sensors to verify the package is present before issuing the token; that adds state-management complexity beyond this interview's scope."
932	  - **The one tricky line** with reasoning — "The `pop` is what makes the code one-time. If we just left it in the map, a replay attack would work the second time. We delete on success AND in `_expire`, so the code is gone in either path."
933	
934	  This paragraph is what makes the section TEACHABLE, not just complete. It reads like hellointerview's prose, not like a code comment.
935	
936	  **6. 🎙️ Out-loud script + ⚠️ Follow-up:** Two short pieces — a 1-2 sentence condensed version of the reasoning paragraph for the candidate to MEMORIZE for the interview, plus the one follow-up question they'll be asked.
937	
938	  > 🎙️ Say: "Lookup, expiry guard, ready guard, then release. The one-time-ness is   > the pop — the code is gone, so it can never be replayed."
939	  > ⚠️ "What if the user enters the code right as it expires?" → "The expiry check   > runs first and calls `_expire`, which reclaims the box and drops the code.   > No window where a stale code still opens a box."
940	
941	The 🎙️ line is DERIVED from the reasoning paragraph — same content, condensed. They are not redundant; the paragraph teaches, the 🎙️ remembers.
942	
943	**APPROACH COMPARISON — for the ONE most interesting helper method only:**
944	Pick the single helper method where the candidate's design has a non-obvious choice (usually `getAvailableCompartment` / `findSpot` / `assignSeat` — the "how do I locate the right resource" method). For THIS one method, after blocks 1-4 above, insert an "### Approach" subsection that walks through 2-3 candidate implementations and EXPLICITLY rejects the first ones. Format strictly:
945	
946	  **Approach 1 — [name] (rejected):**   Pseudocode for the naive version.   > **Challenges:** [the SPECIFIC failure mode that kills this approach — not "it's slow", but "tokens expire 7 days before they're cleaned up, so during that window the compartment looks occupied for assignment but is actually free for new tokens — state divergence"]
947	
948	  **Approach 2 — [name] (rejected for this scale):**   Pseudocode for an over-engineered version (e.g. an indexed `Map<Size, Queue>`).   > **Challenges:** [the SPECIFIC complexity cost — "state lives in two places, synchronisation risk: forget to enqueue on pickup → compartment vanishes from availability"]. Add a one-line rule: "I'd flip to this when [N crosses threshold]."
949	
950	  **Chosen approach — [name]:**   Pseudocode for the one we're going with.   > **Why it wins:** [the SPECIFIC reason — "single source of truth: the compartment owns its own physical state, no synchronisation needed, scan is O(n) but n is tiny"].
951	
952	This subsection is the SENIOR SIGNAL. It shows the candidate considered alternatives and rejected them with reasons — the rule of thumb the interviewer scores on. Only do this for ONE method per problem (the most interesting helper) — more than one becomes noise.
953	
954	**HELPER METHODS — written, not skipped:**
955	After the public methods of the orchestrator, write a small ### subsection for the private helpers (`generateAccessToken`, `clearDeposit`, `_expire`, `_unique_code`). Each gets:
956	  - Role one-liner
957	  - Code block (often 3-5 lines)
958	  - One sentence of prose if the helper has a non-obvious choice (e.g. "Setting expiration to `now + 7.days()` here; the clock is injected so tests can fast-forward")
959	
960	Don't skip these — readers can't run code that doesn't exist. But don't over-explain either; helpers are short for a reason.
961	
962	**SMALL ENTITY CLASSES — single block per file:**
963	Entities like `Compartment`, `Reservation`, `Locker`, `Package` can be presented as a SINGLE ```java block per file (in `model/*.java`) with inline comments — they don't need the 6-block format. The 6-block treatment is for the orchestrator's PUBLIC methods and the one approach-comparison helper.
964	
965	Cover the enums, the interfaces/abstract base classes, the strategies, and the orchestrator. EVERY class shown here must appear in the §4 diagram (and vice-versa).
966	
967	**VERIFICATION — full-lifecycle dry runs (after the orchestrator, before §7):**
968	After all methods are presented, add a "### Verification" subsection that traces 3 scenarios with EXPLICIT before/after state tables. Hellointerview's verification section is the strongest interview signal because it proves the design works on paper before any test runs. Three scenarios:
969	
970	  1. **Happy-path lifecycle** — `assign → deposit → pickup`. Show the state of every data structure (`compartments`, `_reservations`, `_by_code`) before each step and after each step. Use a small table or indented `State:` lines per step.
971	
972	  2. **An expired-pickup attempt** — `assign → deposit → fast-forward clock → pickup`. Show that `pickup` raises and the compartment is correctly reclaimed by `_expire`.
973	
974	  3. **A race-prone scenario in plain English** — without code, describe what would happen WITHOUT the lock and what happens WITH it. This is a teaching dry-run, not a trace; one paragraph max.
975	
976	Format example:
977	> **Scenario 1 — Happy path:**
978	> Initial state: `compartments={A: AVAILABLE, B: AVAILABLE, C: AVAILABLE}`, > `_reservations={}`, `_by_code={}`
979	>
980	> | Step | Call | After |
981	> |---|---|---|
982	> | 1 | `assign_package("LKR1", pkg_med)` | A: AVAILABLE, B: RESERVED, C: AVAILABLE; `_by_code={ "9421": res-1 }` |
983	> | 2 | `deposit_package("res-1")` | B: OCCUPIED; `res-1.status = AWAITING_PICKUP` |
984	> | 3 | `pickup_package("9421")` | B: AVAILABLE; `_by_code={}`; returned package status PICKED_UP |
985	>
986	> Both indexes cleaned up, compartment freed. ✓
987	
988	The verification section bridges §6 (code) and §7 (run) — readers see the design works BEFORE seeing the test output. This is a tutorial-quality step that hellointerview does explicitly and we previously skipped.
989	
990	After the last class, add a **Complexity Summary** table — the interviewer WILL ask this, so have it ready. For every public operation on the orchestrator, list:
991	
992	| Operation | Time Complexity | Space | Key reason |
993	|---|---|---|---|
994	| park(vehicle) | O(log n) | O(1) | heapq push on n available spots |
995	| unpark(ticket_id) | O(log n) | O(1) | dict lookup O(1) + heapq pop O(log n) |
996	| … | … | … | … |
997	
998	Be precise — reference the actual data structure from §5.5-C. This table is what you read out when the interviewer says "what's the time complexity of your design?"
999	
1000	## 7. Putting It Together (verified run + JUnit 5 suite)
1001	TWO deliverables in this section, both run cleanly:
1002	
1003	### 7.1 `main.java` — narrated demo driver
1004	A small `__main__` script that exercises 3-5 happy/tricky cases sequentially with `print()` lines so the candidate (or interviewer) can `mvn compile exec:java -Dexec.mainClass="com.interview.lld.<domain>.Main" -q` and read a human-friendly trace. Each case prints a one-line "Case N <description> -> OK" so a green run is visually obvious. NO assertions hidden in `try/except False` patterns — just `assert X, message` so failures point at the exact line. The driver is for demonstration; rigorous coverage lives in `tests/`.
1005	
1006	### 7.2 `tests/test_<domain>.java` — JUnit 5 suite
1007	Proper JUnit 5 tests under `tests/`. Every public method of the orchestrator gets at least one test; every typed exception path has its own test. Use parametrize for size/state matrices. Use `JUnit 5.raises(<TypedError>)` — never bare `JUnit 5.raises(Exception)`. Inject the clock via a fixture so expiry tests are millisecond-fast. Include:
1008	
1009	  - One test per **happy path** per public operation
1010	  - One test per **typed exception** raised by the orchestrator
1011	  - One **boundary** test (capacity exhausted, empty input, etc.)
1012	  - One **idempotency** test if any method is documented as safe-to-call-twice
1013	  - The **concurrency test** below
1014
